filer: Southern Company Services, Inc.; Alabama Power Company; Georgia Power Company; Mississippi Power Company
overall_summary: Southern Companies support timely and cost-effective integration of large loads while protecting existing customers, and they present updated data showing rapid connections under their state-regulated, vertically-integrated planning model. They urge the Commission to preserve well-functioning state-led frameworks and avoid a one-size-fits-all rule, directing any near-term federal reforms toward regions where wholesale market structures and Commission-jurisdictional processes are directly implicated.

quotes.text: Southern Companies re-emphasize their support for timely and cost-effective integration of large-load customers to support growth and enable economic development and bolster the nation’s national security, in a way that protects existing customers, as called for in the Secretary of Energy’s Advanced Notice of Proposed Rulemaking
quotes.concern: Supports timely large-load integration that protects existing customers, per the ANOPR

quotes.text: Southern Companies are connecting large load retail customers quickly and efficiently, while protecting customers from cost-shifting, as identified in the ANOPR and the President’s Ratepayer Protection Pledge
quotes.concern: Claims success connecting large loads quickly while protecting customers from cost-shifting

quotes.text: Southern Companies respectfully request that the Commission allow Southern Companies to continue building on this record of success and progress towards the ANOPR’s goals, while preserving the historic jurisdiction of our state regulators over retail customer connections, the terms and conditions of which are inextricably embedded in our bundled rate mechanisms.
quotes.concern: Asks the Commission to preserve state jurisdiction over retail large-load connections

quotes.text: As of April 2026, 32 large load customers have committed to receive approximately 15,600 MW of electric service from Georgia Power, including executed Contracts for Electric Service for 9,572 MW of large load.
quotes.concern: Provides updated data on large-load commitments to demonstrate success

quotes.text: Southern Companies explained that their vertically-integrated structure and integrated resource planning process enable them to plan for and serve large load customers by holistically combining generation, fuel, transmission, and distribution planning.
quotes.concern: Credits vertically-integrated structure and integrated resource planning for serving large loads

quotes.text: Georgia Power Company has implemented several mechanisms aimed to protect existing customers, including minimum bill obligations for large loads, long-term contractual commitments, collateral requirements, termination protection, and regulatory review and accountability for customer-specific arrangements.
quotes.concern: Lists customer-protection mechanisms such as minimum bills, collateral, and termination protection

quotes.text: Southern Companies’ experience demonstrates that coordinated state-regulated planning structures can support more timely generation development and reduce interconnection delays relative to regions currently experiencing substantial queue backlogs and infrastructure constraints.  These differing regional conditions support preserving flexibility for frameworks already providing coordinated and timely infrastructure development.
quotes.concern: Argues state-regulated planning reduces delays and warrants preserving flexibility

quotes.text: Southern Companies have integrated large volumes of new loads in a manner that not only protects existing customers from additional costs, but actually benefits these existing customers through allocating fixed-cost recovery across a broader customer base.
quotes.concern: Claims large-load integration benefits existing customers by spreading fixed costs

quotes.text: We respectfully suggest that any federal initiative that comes from this proceeding preserve well-functioning state-led frameworks that can help deliver speed and affordability.
quotes.concern: Urges any federal action to preserve well-functioning state-led frameworks

quotes.text: Given the success of state-regulated integrated resource planning and bundled retail service frameworks in integrating large load customers, such as in Southern Companies’ territories, a one-size-fits-all rule would be counterproductive.
quotes.concern: Opposes a one-size-fits-all federal rule given state-level success

quotes.text: Any federal reforms adopted in this proceeding should preserve the flexibility for states and vertically integrated utilities to continue to use coordinated bundled-service structures that are already delivering timely infrastructure development, customer protections, and reliable service.
quotes.concern: Asks that reforms preserve flexibility for states and vertically integrated utilities

quotes.text: To the extent the Commission determines that additional near-term federal reforms are appropriate, those reforms should focus principally on regions where wholesale market structures, transmission service constructs, and Commission-jurisdictional processes are directly implicated.
quotes.concern: Suggests near-term reforms target RTO/market regions, not vertically integrated areas

bins.name: Protecting existing customers from cost-shifting
bins.description: Southern Companies say they integrate large loads while protecting existing customers from cost-shifting, using minimum bills, collateral, and termination protection. They argue this approach can actually benefit existing customers by spreading fixed-cost recovery across a broader base.

bins.name: Existing-customer protection mechanisms
bins.description: The filing details state-approved mechanisms that shield existing customers from large-load costs, including minimum bill obligations, collateral requirements, and termination protection. It frames these as proven safeguards against subsidization.

bins.name: Collateral and contract commitments
bins.description: Southern Companies cite collateral requirements and long-term contractual commitments among the tools that protect existing customers from large-load risk.

bins.name: Preserve state retail jurisdiction
bins.description: Southern Companies ask the Commission to preserve the historic jurisdiction of state regulators over retail large-load connections, whose terms are embedded in bundled rate mechanisms. They want federal action to defer to well-functioning state-led frameworks.

bins.name: Integrated resource planning model
bins.description: The filing credits its vertically-integrated structure and integrated resource planning for holistically combining generation, fuel, transmission, and distribution to serve large loads.

bins.name: Preserve flexibility, no uniform rule
bins.description: Southern Companies argue regional differences justify preserving flexibility for frameworks already delivering timely infrastructure, and that a one-size-fits-all rule would be counterproductive. They suggest any near-term reforms focus on regions where wholesale markets and Commission-jurisdictional processes are directly implicated.

bins.name: Targeted near-term reforms only
bins.description: Southern Companies accept that targeted near-term reforms may be appropriate, but only for RTO/market regions, not for state-regulated vertically integrated territories.

bins.name: Speed to power for large loads
bins.description: The filing emphasizes connecting large loads quickly and efficiently as a shared goal aligned with the ANOPR and the Ratepayer Protection Pledge.

bins.name: Bundled retail service authority
bins.description: Southern Companies stress that retail connection terms are inextricably embedded in their bundled rate mechanisms under state oversight, which they want preserved.

bins.name: Updated large-load success data
bins.description: The filing supplements the record with current figures, including 32 large-load customers committing to about 15,600 MW and 9,572 MW under executed contracts, to show its model is working.

bins.name: State-led integrated planning benefits
bins.description: Southern Companies argue coordinated state-regulated planning supports timely generation development and reduces interconnection delays compared with regions facing queue backlogs.
